1: A theme: 'so these three men stopped answering Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes' — the paralysis of conventional wisdom before an anomalous case. The three friends could not answer because their theology lacked the categories for innocent suffering. The NT supplies the missing category: the suffering of the righteous is redemptive, not merely disciplinary, when joined to Christ's.
2: A theme: Elihu's anger 'burned against Job, because he declared himself righteous rather than God' — the accusation that Job's insistence on his own righteousness displaces God's. The NT navigates this tension: the doctrine of justification holds that the sinner is declared righteous not by his own insistence but by God's declaration through Christ. Job's self-defense, vindicated in Job 42:7, is not self-righteousness — it is honest assessment of his actual integrity.
3: A revelation of God: Elihu 'was also angry with the three friends because they had found no answer and yet had condemned Job' — he perceives the injustice of condemnation without proof. The NT's standard: 'judge not by appearances, but judge with right judgment' (John 7:24). The friends condemned without evidence; Elihu at least recognizes that evidence has not been produced.
4: A theme: Elihu 'had waited to speak... because the others were older than he' — deference to age as a wisdom convention. The NT both honors and qualifies this: 'do not let anyone despise you for your youth' (1 Timothy 4:12); 'out of the mouth of infants and nursing babies you have prepared praise' (Matthew 21:16). Wisdom is not the exclusive possession of the aged.
5: A theme: 'when Elihu saw that the three men had no answer, his anger was aroused' — the recognition that the conventional wisdom tradition has exhausted itself. The NT describes a similar moment: the religious leaders, questioned by Jesus about the baptism of John, 'knew not what to answer' (Mark 11:33). When conventional frameworks fail to answer, a new word is needed.
6: A theme: 'I am young and you are old; that is why I was afraid and held back' — the fear of speaking out of proper order. The NT's prophetic pattern overrides social hierarchy when the Spirit impels: 'when they arrest you and deliver you over, do not be anxious beforehand what you are to say, but say whatever is given you in that hour' (Mark 13:11). The Spirit speaks through those who defer, when deference reaches its limit.
7: A theme: 'I thought, let the aged speak; let many years teach wisdom' — the assumption that age accumulates wisdom. The NT confirms the honor due to elders (1 Timothy 5:17) while also holding that wisdom is a gift of the Spirit, not a product of years (1 Corinthians 12:8). Both are true; neither is absolute.
8: A revelation of God: 'but it is the Spirit in a person — the breath of the Almighty — that gives them understanding' — the Spirit as the giver of wisdom, independent of human credentials. The NT's Pentecost theology is the full expression of this: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh, and your sons and daughters shall prophesy' (Acts 2:17). The understanding Elihu describes as the Spirit's gift to individuals becomes, at Pentecost, the Spirit's gift to the whole community.
9: A revelation of God: 'it is not the great who are always wise, nor the aged who always understand' — the democratization of wisdom through the Spirit. The NT's corroboration: 'God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong' (1 Corinthians 1:27). The Spirit bypasses credentials and status.
10: A theme: 'therefore I say: listen to me; I too will express what I think' — the claim to a hearing on the basis of Spirit-given insight rather than social status. The NT's pattern of prophetic speech is similar: 'we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard' (Acts 4:20). The compulsion to speak comes from the reality of what has been received, not from earned authority.
11: A theme: 'I listened carefully to your arguments while you searched for what to say' — the patient attending to all that has been said before adding one's own voice. The NT's wisdom: 'let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger' (James 1:19). Elihu embodies the listening before speaking that wisdom requires.
12: A revelation of God: 'not one of you has refuted Job or answered what he said' — the honest assessment that conventional theology has not provided a real answer. The NT identifies the real answer: the cross and resurrection supply what no theodicy argument can — not an explanation of suffering but the presence of God in suffering and the power of God through it.
13: A theme: 'do not say, we have found wisdom; let God deal with him' — the warning against deflecting to God as a way of avoiding the hard intellectual work. The NT's parallel: the disciples who asked 'who sinned, this man or his parents?' (John 9:2) were similarly attempting to route the uncomfortable case into a pre-formed theological category. Jesus rejected the deflection and addressed the situation directly.
14: A theme: 'he has not directed his argument against me, and I will not reply to him with your kind of arguments' — Elihu's commitment to engage afresh rather than repeat the failed approach. The NT's pattern: Jesus taught 'as one who had authority, and not as their scribes' (Matthew 7:29) — not reprising the existing framework but speaking from a different ground entirely.
15: A revelation of God: 'they are astonished; they say nothing more; words have left them' — the exhaustion of human wisdom before the problem of innocent suffering. The NT's response is not a better argument but a person: 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Hebrews 1:2). When words have left human wisdom, God speaks his final word in flesh.
16: A theme: 'I waited, but they did not speak; they stood there and answered no more' — the silence that precedes the new voice. In the NT, silence precedes the new word: the silence of Zechariah before the angel (Luke 1:20), the silence of Jesus before Pilate (Matthew 27:14), and ultimately the silence of the tomb before Easter morning. Silence is not the end; it is the space before the decisive speech.
17: A theme: 'I too will give my answer; I too will state what I think' — the moment of prophetic declaration. The NT holds that Spirit-impelled speech is not merely opinion-sharing: 'the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say' (Luke 12:12). Elihu's 'what I think' is not mere opinion; he believes he speaks by the Spirit (Job 32:8).
18: A revelation of God: 'I am full of words; the Spirit within me urges me on' — the prophetic compulsion to speak. The NT's parallel: Jeremiah's 'there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones' (Jeremiah 20:9); the disciples who 'cannot but speak' (Acts 4:20). The Spirit's fullness presses outward; the contained word demands release.
19: A shadow: 'my belly is like wine with no outlet — like new wineskins about to burst' — the pressure of uncontained speech. The NT's new wine imagery: 'no one puts new wine into old wineskins. If he does, the wine will burst the skins' (Mark 2:22). The new teaching that Jesus brings has exactly this character — it cannot be contained in the old forms. Elihu's image anticipates the NT's description of the gospel's pressure on old structures.
20: A theme: 'I will speak and find relief; I will open my lips and answer' — the therapeutic necessity of faithful speech. The NT's parallel is testimony: 'for with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved' (Romans 10:10). Speaking what one has received is not just transmission but a form of receiving it more fully.
21: A revelation of God: 'I will show partiality to no one, nor will I flatter anyone' — the impartiality that the Spirit-given voice must maintain. The NT grounds divine impartiality in God's own character: 'God shows no partiality' (Romans 2:11; Acts 10:34). The judge of all is impartial; the witness who speaks for him must be as well.
22: A revelation of God: 'for I do not know how to flatter; if I did, my Maker would soon remove me' — the accountability of the prophetic witness before his Maker. The NT's equivalent: 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body' (2 Corinthians 5:10). The one who flatters rather than speaks truth has disqualified himself before his Maker.
